text.title: Content Audit Process Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Content Audit Process application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The audit MUST cover EVERY content URL in scope. Sampling is rejected (audits exist to find the buried bad pages).
rule.rationale: A sampled audit hides the systemic worst pages, which are precisely the ones the audit exists to find.

rule.statement: Every item MUST be scored against ≥4 criteria: accuracy, traffic, conversion, freshness (+ optional SEO, brand fit). Single-criterion audits are rejected.
rule.rationale: Single-criterion (e.g., traffic-only) audits delete legally-required low-traffic pages or keep high-traffic outdated ones.

rule.statement: Each item MUST receive exactly one action: Keep | Update | Consolidate | Rewrite | Remove | Review. Untyped actions are rejected.
rule.rationale: Typed actions map to downstream owners; untyped actions stall.

rule.statement: The action plan MUST rank items by impact × effort. Unranked plans are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without ranking the team executes the easy fixes; the high-impact ones rot.

rule.statement: Items under legal hold MUST be tagged and excluded from Remove actions. Removing legal-hold content is a hard fail.
rule.rationale: Removing legal-hold content is a regulatory exposure; the tag must prevent the action.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
